jokes.body: I told my doctor I broke my arm in two places.
jokes.punch: He told me to stop going to those places.

jokes.body: I used to hate facial hair, but then it grew on me.
A support group for people who can't stop narrating their own lives. Nobody speaks — they just describe the silence.

variants.body: Cemeteries raised burial prices. Blamed cost of living.
A cemetery quietly raised its burial prices this quarter and, when asked to explain, cited the rising cost of living.

jokes.body: A cemetery raised its burial prices and blamed the cost of living.
Self-checkout machines have made us all unpaid employees who get to watch our groceries slowly betray us.
Why did the nihilist cross the road?
jokes.punch: It doesn't matter.

jokes.body: A horror movie but it's just a guy who can't remember if he locked his door, played in real time for 90 minutes.
My therapist told me I have trouble accepting things I can't control.
jokes.punch: We'll see about that.

jokes.body: I'm reading a book about anti-gravity.
jokes.punch: It's impossible to put down.

jokes.body: The most haunted thing in any horror movie is never the ghost — it's the mortgage.
Life is like a box of chocolates.
jokes.punch: It's shorter for fat people.

jokes.body: She will be a heroine.
jokes.punch: Not a hero — but a heroine addict's best friend.

jokes.body: Gayest person award goes to... Homo...
jokes.punch: ...Sapiens.

jokes.body: I just dropped a deuce on Trump...
jokes.punch: ...Tower. In the public bathroom.

jokes.body: Humans are passive plagiarists — we drink from the urethra of ignorance (collective data) and piss out a spin. We don't create, we recombine.
NYPD as a homeless escort patrol. The crime isn't being homeless — it's looking homeless. They're not protecting the public from crime, they're protecting aesthetics from poverty.
Prompt engineering as bending digital reality before the Fun Police arrive to shut it down. The wizard who codes the rules of the game from inside the game.
Brain not keeping up with a conversation — you throw an unbaked cookie into the mix just to say something. The half-baked contribution as social survival.
Politicians use a dusting of truth to pass the cover-book bullshit test. The winding road of bullshit is paved with 10% fact and 90% confident misdirection.

variants.body: 'Stew Pitt Nay Grow'
'Imis Pen, Ice Liquor'

jokes.body: Auditory code: 'Emma Lion Pizza Sheet' — say it fast.
jokes.punch: I'm a lying piece of shit.

jokes.body: Ambiguity Juxtaposition: two conversations happening simultaneously — find the equivocation overlap point where both meanings collide.
Frame Hijacking: mirror an insult back so the target can't tell if you're agreeing or escalating. 'Why, because my balls are bigger than yours?'
The Dundee Award: bestow an absurd, unearned honorific on someone to confuse or disarm. 'As the recipient of the 2024 Dundee Prize for Excellence in Being Wrong...'
The Telescope/Salt Shaker: physical miming of an O-shape leading to an x-rated visual misunderstanding.
'It's Rapetime!' — corrected to 'Harvey Time' (Weinstein). The dark alley bit.
Crass Rebranding: 'Constabulary' → 'Come stab u later'. 'Aspergers' → 'Assbergers'.
The Litter of Babies: gross-out lines engineered to guarantee a hurling reaction from women.
Incongruity-Resolution: brain perceives two incompatible scripts. The joke is the logical mechanism that resolves them. The laugh is the release of that tension.
Backloading: the reveal word always goes at the END of the sentence. Never step on the laugh by burying the funny word mid-sentence.
Semantic Fan-Out: pick a word like 'Run' or 'Dates' — list every possible definition — find the switch point where meanings collide unexpectedly.
The Purple Elephant: call out the antisocial pink elephant in the room without making the target an outcast. Comedy as social surgery.
Why did the little girl fall off the swing?
jokes.punch: Because she had no arms.

jokes.body: I spilled spot remover on my dog.
jokes.punch: Now he's gone.
jokes.context: Steven Wright. Takes the literal interpretation of a product name and applies it to a physical entity — absurdist erasure.
jokes.notes: Deadpan delivery essential. No setup signaling.

jokes.body: I busted a mirror and got seven years bad luck.
jokes.punch: But my lawyer thinks he can get me five.
jokes.context: Steven Wright. Blends folklore/superstition with banality of the modern legal system.
jokes.notes: The word 'five' lands the joke — pure backloading.

jokes.body: Everywhere is within walking distance...
jokes.punch: ...if you have the time.
jokes.context: Steven Wright. Tautology masquerading as profound realization — technically true, practically useless.

jokes.body: If at first you don't succeed...
jokes.punch: ...then skydiving isn't for you.
jokes.context: Steven Wright. Paraprosdokian — subverts motivational cliché by applying it to a high-stakes scenario where failure is instantly fatal.
jokes.notes: Classic backloading. 'Skydiving' does all the work.

jokes.body: I put instant coffee in a microwave...
jokes.punch: ...and almost went back in time.
jokes.context: Steven Wright. Surrealism — combines two symbols of modern convenience to suggest exponential accumulation of speed.

jokes.body: I'm sick of following my dreams.
jokes.punch: I'm just going to ask them where they're going and hook up with them later.
jokes.context: Mitch Hedberg. Personifies the abstract concept of a 'dream,' treating an existential pursuit as a casual social appointment.
jokes.notes: The delivery drag on 'later' matters enormously.

jokes.body: I don't have a girlfriend.
jokes.punch: I just have someone who would be really mad if they heard me say that.
jokes.context: Mitch Hedberg. Syntactical evasion — redefines a romantic relationship purely through the lens of conflict avoidance.

jokes.body: I know I'm ugly. When my dog found out we look alike...
jokes.punch: ...he killed himself.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. Self-deprecating. Hyperbolic — attributes complex psychological vanity and suicidal ideation to an animal.

jokes.body: My wife's a terrible cook. If I leave dental floss in the kitchen...
jokes.punch: ...the cockroaches hang themselves.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. Escalates marital complaints into the grotesque to emphasize a toxic domestic environment.

jokes.body: I went to a fight the other night...
jokes.punch: ...and a hockey game broke out.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. Structural inversion — swaps the primary and secondary events of a familiar observation.

jokes.body: I rang up British Telecom and said: 'I want to report a nuisance caller.'
jokes.punch: He said: 'Not you again.'
jokes.context: Tim Vine. Reverses the subject-object relationship — audience assumes the comedian is the victim, but he's the perpetrator.

jokes.body: Here's a picture of me with REM.
jokes.punch: That's me in the corner.
jokes.context: Milton Jones. Relies on shared cultural knowledge of 'Losing My Religion' by REM — 'That's me in the corner' is a lyric.

jokes.body: Two fish in a tank. One says:
jokes.punch: 'How do you drive this thing?'
jokes.context: Peter Kay. Lexical ambiguity pun — pivots on the dual meaning of 'tank' (fish tank vs. military tank).

jokes.body: If God had written the Bible, the first line should have been:
jokes.punch: 'It's round.'
jokes.context: Eddie Izzard. Satirizes religious resistance to science — a divine author would have prioritized basic cosmology.

jokes.body: I worry about ridiculous things... how does a guy who drives a snowplough...
jokes.punch: ...get to work in the morning?
jokes.context: Billy Connolly. Highlights logistical paradoxes of modern infrastructure and the circular dependency of emergency services.

jokes.body: I used to go out with a giraffe. People said 'he takes up too much room'...
jokes.punch: 'Well he can take his hat off for a start!'
jokes.context: Paul Merton. Introduces absurd premise but grounds the conflict in mundane cinematic etiquette.

jokes.body: We weren't very religious. On Hanukkah...
jokes.punch: ...my mother had our menorah on a dimmer.
jokes.context: Richard Lewis. Synthesizes modern electrical convenience with ancient tradition to illustrate lack of orthodox commitment.

jokes.body: The first guy who persuaded a blind person they needed sunglasses...
jokes.punch: ...he must have been a hell of a salesman.
jokes.context: Jimmy Carr. Explores absurdity of an accepted social norm by pointing out a logical inconsistency.

jokes.body: My girlfriend is absolutely beautiful. Body like a Greek statue —
jokes.punch: ...completely pale, no arms.
jokes.context: Phil Wang. Uses a common simile then pivots to the literal, ruined physical state of actual antiquities.
jokes.notes: The pause before 'completely pale' sets up the misdirection.

jokes.body: A 90-year-old's friends hire a lady of the evening. She says: 'I'm here for super sex.' He replies:
jokes.punch: 'I'll take the soup.'
jokes.context: Jane Lynch. Pivots on phonetic ambiguity ('super sex' vs 'soup or sex') and subversion of virility tropes in aging.

jokes.body: A string tries to get a drink at a bar. He comes back frayed and says:
jokes.punch: 'No, I'm a frayed knot.'
jokes.context: Kumail Nanjiani. Classic pun — homophonic relationship between 'afraid not' and the physical state of frayed string.

jokes.body: Rene Descartes is asked if he wants an hors d'oeuvre. He says: 'I think not'...
jokes.punch: ...and disappears.
jokes.context: Aasif Mandvi. Intellectual pun requiring Cartesian philosophy — 'I think therefore I am.' By ceasing to think, he ceases to exist.

jokes.body: Ron Swanson on eating at a bowling alley:
jokes.punch: 'When I eat, it is the food that is scared.'
jokes.context: Mike Schur (Parks & Rec). Personifies sustenance to establish hyper-masculinity — transforms passive act of eating into domination.

jokes.body: A man pushes another off a bridge after learning he belongs to a different Baptist council from 1912.
jokes.context: Eugene Mirman. Satirizes the narcissism of minor differences — how violent hatred stems from imperceptible theological distinctions.

jokes.body: My father was a night watchman, but he was a victim of technology.
jokes.punch: He was replaced by a lock.
jokes.context: Colin Quinn. Highlights absurdity of a human occupation so simple it can be replaced by a basic mechanical object.

jokes.body: The Olympic Silver Medal. Of all the losers...
jokes.punch: ...you came in first! You're the number one loser!
jokes.context: Jerry Seinfeld. Inverts traditional hierarchies — identifies the unique agony of absolute proximity to ultimate victory.

jokes.body: Hot Pockets. I paid for that? It's frozen.
jokes.punch: But it can be served boiling lava hot.
jokes.context: Jim Gaffigan. Commentary on post-consumption regret and inherent contradictions of modern processed food.

jokes.body: The only knife I have seen in Hampstead...
jokes.punch: ...was a palette knife held by an oil painter.
jokes.context: Ricky Gervais. Generates humor from the perspective of an outsider observing the absolute absence of crime in elite circles.

jokes.body: I think all bullets should cost five thousand dollars.
jokes.punch: 'Man, I was mad, but not five-thousand-dollar mad.'
jokes.context: Chris Rock. Reframes violent social crisis through the lens of capitalism and supply-and-demand.

jokes.body: Dogs are the leaders of the planet. If you see two life forms, one of them's making a poop, the other one's carrying it for him,
jokes.punch: who would you assume is in charge?
jokes.context: Jerry Seinfeld. Reframes a mundane daily activity (picking up dog waste) to invert the assumed power dynamic between owner and pet.

jokes.body: In terms of instant relief,
jokes.punch: canceling plans is like heroin.
jokes.context: John Mulaney. Hyperbolic comparison that validates universal social anxiety — the relief of cancellation is pharmacological in intensity.

jokes.body: Have you ever noticed that anybody driving slower than you is an idiot,
jokes.punch: and anyone going faster than you is a maniac?
jokes.context: George Carlin. Exposes the self-centered nature of human perception — everyone's own speed is the rational norm.

jokes.body: There is no 'we' in fries. I strongly believe that. 'We should get some fries.'
jokes.punch: No, I am getting fries, you can get your own.
jokes.context: Jim Gaffigan. Defends personal food autonomy with mock philosophical conviction — exposes how 'we' is used to normalize food theft.

jokes.body: I'm not a very good reader. I just realized I bought a book on 'How to Read.'
jokes.punch: It's like buying a blank canvas with a set of paints and the instructions say, 'Paint the instructions first.'
jokes.context: Brian Regan. Self-deprecating observational loop — the solution requires the skill it's meant to teach.

jokes.body: If you want to know what God thinks of money,
jokes.punch: just look at the people he gave it to.
jokes.context: Wanda Sykes. Theological irony — uses divine attribution to land a pointed class critique.

jokes.body: I haven't slept for ten days,
jokes.punch: because that would be too long.
jokes.context: Mitch Hedberg. Classic Hedberg logic-trap — reframes the premise as a duration problem rather than a health one.
jokes.notes: Deadpan delivery essential. No setup signaling.

jokes.body: I intend to live forever.
jokes.punch: So far, so good.
jokes.context: Steven Wright. Logical fallacy delivered as earnest progress report — applies milestone thinking to an impossible goal.

jokes.body: A lot of people don't like bumper stickers. I don't mind bumper stickers. To me a bumper sticker is a shortcut.
jokes.punch: It's like a little sign that says 'Hey, let's never hang out.'
jokes.context: Demetri Martin. Reframes a decorative object as a social screening tool — the joke lands on the word 'shortcut' then pays it off.

jokes.body: I bought some batteries,
jokes.punch: but they weren't included.
jokes.context: Jimmy Carr. Exploits the familiar packaging phrase 'batteries not included' by reversing the context — the batteries ARE the purchase.

jokes.body: My mother always used to say: The older you get, the better you get.
jokes.punch: Unless you're a banana.
jokes.context: Anthony Jeselnik. Classic paraprosdokian — warm maternal wisdom undercut by a single absurd exception that reframes everything.

jokes.body: It says here in this history book that luckily, the good guys have won every single time.
jokes.punch: What are the odds?
jokes.context: Norm Macdonald. Deadpan skewering of historical revisionism — history is always written by the winners, so the outcome is 100% predictable.

jokes.body: I'm not a fatalist,
jokes.punch: but even if I were, what could I do about it?
jokes.context: Emo Philips. Philosophical trap — denying fatalism while structurally demonstrating it. The punchline is the proof of the premise.

jokes.body: A day without sunshine is like,
jokes.punch: you know, night.
jokes.context: Steve Martin. Subverts the inspirational cliché by completing it with an obvious, literal answer — the emptiness is the point.

jokes.body: Before you criticize someone, you should walk a mile in their shoes. That way when you criticize them,
jokes.punch: you are a mile away from them and you have their shoes.
jokes.context: Jack Handey. Takes the empathy cliché literally and extracts a tactical advantage — the punchline is logistical, not moral.

jokes.body: Guns don't kill people, people kill people.
jokes.punch: And monkeys do too (if they have a gun).
jokes.context: Eddie Izzard. Adds a technically accurate exception to a political platitude — the absurdity of the qualifier exposes the absurdity of the original.

jokes.body: I have a lot of growing up to do.
jokes.punch: I realized that the other day inside my fort.
jokes.context: Zach Galifianakis. Self-deprecation via setting — the revelation of immaturity happens in the least mature possible location.

jokes.body: I'm a vegetarian. I'm not strict,
jokes.punch: I eat pork, beef, and chicken. But I'm not a strict vegetarian.
jokes.context: Jay London. Hollow self-labeling — the qualifier 'strict' is used to absorb the entire contradiction rather than resolve it.

jokes.body: I told my psychiatrist that everyone hates me.
jokes.punch: He said I was being ridiculous — everyone hasn't met me yet.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. The therapist's correction makes things worse — reframes the universal rejection as not yet complete.

jokes.body: A study says that having a lot of friends makes you live longer.
jokes.punch: Which explains why I'm going to die on Thursday.
jokes.context: Conan O'Brien. Applies a statistical finding to a specific personal timeline — the precision of 'Thursday' sells it.

jokes.body: I don't exercise.
jokes.punch: If God had wanted me to bend over, he would have put diamonds on the floor.
jokes.context: Joan Rivers. Theological justification for laziness — elevates personal sloth to a matter of divine intent.

jokes.body: I'm in my late thirties, which is the age where you have to start doing things like 'taking care of your body'
jokes.punch: because otherwise, you die.
jokes.context: Mike Birbiglia. Strips away euphemism — the vague cultural pressure to 'take care of yourself' is bluntly literalized as survival.

jokes.body: I want my children to have all the things I couldn't afford.
jokes.punch: Then I want to move in with them.
jokes.context: Phyllis Diller. Generational sacrifice reframed as a long-game financial strategy — parental love as delayed self-interest.

jokes.body: I'm at that age where my friends are either getting married or getting DUIs, and it's like, which path am I going down?
jokes.punch: Obviously neither, I'm staying inside.
jokes.context: Taylor Tomlinson. Sets up a binary life choice then rejects both — the third option is social withdrawal, delivered as contentment.

jokes.body: Outside of a dog, a book is man's best friend.
jokes.punch: Inside of a dog, it's too dark to read.
jokes.context: Groucho Marx. Classic paraprosdokian — 'outside of' is idiomatic in the setup but then taken spatially/literally in the punchline.

jokes.body: I'm giving up acting to become a monk.
jokes.punch: I've had it with this habit.
jokes.context: Gary Delaney. 'Habit' does double duty — monastic robe and a thing one is fed up with. The decision to become a monk generates the punchline word.

jokes.body: I decided to sell my Hoover.
jokes.punch: Well, it was just collecting dust.
jokes.context: Tim Vine. 'Collecting dust' is both the literal failure of a vacuum cleaner and the idiom for abandonment — the punchline is the reason and the joke simultaneously.

jokes.body: I used to be a plastic surgeon.
jokes.punch: Which just goes to show, you never know what someone is made of.
jokes.context: Stewart Francis. 'What someone is made of' is idiomatic (character/resolve) but here cashed out literally — a plastic surgeon knows exactly what people are made of.

jokes.body: About a month before he died, my grandfather covered his back with lard.
jokes.punch: After that, he went downhill fast.
jokes.context: Milton Jones. 'Went downhill fast' is idiomatic (deteriorated quickly) but the lard on the back generates a literal physical image of lubricated downward momentum.

jokes.body: To err is human,
jokes.punch: but it feels divine.
jokes.context: Mae West. Inverts Pope's 'To err is human, to forgive divine' — strips the moral imperative and replaces it with sensual pleasure. The pivot is on 'divine' shifting from theological to hedonistic.

variants.body: Technically they gave you the gift of time. Ungratefully.
I immediately texted back 'I also cancel. I cancel our friendship. I cancel the concept of plans. I cancel Tuesday.'
I already knew. I dressed up anyway. Sat in the restaurant alone. Ordered for two. Ate both. Left a review: 'great for one.'
They cancelled because they could tell I was going to be weird tonight. They sensed it. I was going to be VERY weird.
Honestly? Best case scenario. Now I can cancel something ELSE.

jokes.body: Someone cancels plans last minute.
jokes.context: Riff scenario: respond to 'they cancelled on me' in five silly directions. Each variant is a different reactive impulse, not a better version — just a different weird angle.

variants.body: Studies show walking through a doorway resets short-term memory. I use this as an excuse. Studies didn't say that. I made that up. I'm in a doorway.
I stand there. Long enough that it becomes a new thing. Now I live here. This is my doorway now.
I start listing everything I could have wanted. 'Snack? No. Vengeance? Possibly. To yell at no one? Getting warmer.'
Maybe there was no reason. Maybe the journey was the destination. Maybe I'm a cat.
I go back to where I started. Remember. Go back. Forget again. I have done this eleven times. I live between two rooms now.

jokes.body: You walk into a room and forget why you're there.
jokes.context: Riff scenario: the doorway memory wipe. Five silly directions from the same moment of standing in a room with no idea why.

jokes.body: Conversation is not a delivery system for facts. It's a game of angles — find the weird corner, the sideways door, the floor that's actually a ceiling. Stop reporting what's true. Start playing with what's possible. The funniest people aren't funnier because they know more. They're funnier because they stopped taking the obvious path the moment it became obvious. Every straight line is an invitation to curve. Every serious thing is a dare. You already know how to bullshit — you've been doing it socially to survive since childhood. Now do it on purpose, with craft, with speed. The lie that makes someone laugh is more honest than the fact that makes them glaze over. Riff. Drift. Misread on purpose. See what breaks.
jokes.context: On the art of comedic thinking: abandoning the factual reflex in favour of creative deflection, misdirection, and productive nonsense. This is the core muscle riffing builds.
jokes.notes: Read slowly. Let it land. Come back to it before a writing session.

jokes.body: The factual reflex is the enemy of funny.
jokes.punch: When your brain serves up the literal answer, that's your cue to look for the exit. The joke is never what actually happened — it's the version that shouldn't have happened but somehow makes more sense.
jokes.context: Core technique: override the instinct to report truth. Comedy lives one step sideways from the accurate answer.

jokes.body: Listening to a hobo on the subway explaining his philosophy: everybody is dirty, so he doesn't shower — he's actually cleaner that way. Circular logic as lifestyle. The man has out-reasoned hygiene.
jokes.context: Raw field note. The hobo has constructed an internally consistent worldview from a false premise. That's either insanity or enlightenment and the line is thin.

jokes.body: Just because you are offended,
jokes.punch: doesn't mean you are right.
jokes.context: Ricky Gervais. Dismantles the fallacy that emotional reaction constitutes moral authority. Offense as argument is a non-sequitur.

jokes.body: I've always been fascinated by the fact that you can buy a book on how to be a hypochondriac.
jokes.context: Ricky Gervais. The premise contains its own paradox — a hypochondriac reading about hypochondria will immediately acquire every symptom described.

jokes.body: Animals are not here for us to do as we please with. We are not their superiors, we are their equals. We are their family. Be kind to them.
jokes.punch: Also, they are delicious.
jokes.context: Ricky Gervais. Classic paraprosdokian — warm moral argument completely undercut by the final clause. The punchline doesn't contradict the sentiment, it just complicates it.

jokes.body: I see a lot of people saying, 'I'm spiritual, I don't believe in organized religion.'
jokes.punch: That just means you want to sleep in on Sundays.
jokes.context: Ricky Gervais. Deflates a self-congratulatory identity claim to its bare practical motivation.

jokes.body: If you don't want to hear the answer, don't ask the question.
jokes.punch: Especially if the question is, 'Does my bum look big in this?'
jokes.context: Ricky Gervais. Universal principle made vivid by the most universally dreaded specific case.

jokes.body: Everything is amazing right now and nobody's happy. People come back from flights and tell you their story: 'It was the worst day of my life. We sat on the runway for forty minutes.'
jokes.punch: Oh, really? Did you fly through the air incredibly, like a bird? Did you partake in the miracle of human flight, you non-contributing zero?!
jokes.context: Louis C.K. Gratitude deficit — scales a trivial complaint against the backdrop of technological miracle to expose how comprehensively ungrateful modern life is.
jokes.notes: The escalation to 'non-contributing zero' is the key — it's not gentle.

jokes.body: I have a lot of beliefs... and I live by none of them. That's just the way I am. They are just my beliefs.
jokes.punch: I just like believing them. They are my little believies.
jokes.context: Louis C.K. Honest confession of moral hypocrisy delivered without shame. 'Believies' is the comic diminutive that transforms conviction into a comfort object.

jokes.body: 'I'm bored' is a useless thing to say. You live in a great, big, vast world that you've seen none of. Even the inside of your own mind is endless.
jokes.punch: The fact that you're alive is amazing, so you don't get to say 'I'm bored.'
jokes.context: Louis C.K. Existential rebuke — frames boredom not as an emotion but as an act of ingratitude against the sheer improbability of consciousness.

jokes.body: I went to the bank and the teller said, 'Your account is overdrawn, you have negative ten dollars.'
jokes.punch: How can I have negative money? That means I don't even have no money. I have less than nothing. If a guy with no money walks in here, he's ten dollars richer than me.
jokes.context: Louis C.K. Financial absurdism — follows the logic of negative numbers to their existential conclusion. Poverty is reframed as a philosophical condition.

jokes.body: I don't have a gun, but if I did,
jokes.punch: I would shoot the radio every time they play the '1-877-Kars-4-Kids' commercial.
jokes.context: Louis C.K. Extreme escalation from reasonable irritation — the violence is disproportionate but anyone who has heard that jingle understands the impulse completely.

jokes.body: I get no respect. The way my luck is running,
jokes.punch: if I was a politician I would be honest.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. The 'no respect' frame is flipped — here honesty in politics is framed as a curse, implying integrity is the worst possible electoral strategy.

jokes.body: I told my doctor, 'Every time I look in the mirror, I feel like throwing up. What's wrong with me?'
jokes.punch: He said, 'I don't know, but your eyesight is perfect.'
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. The doctor's response validates the complaint while turning it into a compliment — perfect eyesight as a backhanded insult.

jokes.body: My wife and I were happy for twenty years.
jokes.punch: Then we met.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. Ultra-compressed paraprosdokian — reverses the expected timeline. The punchline implies the happiness was the illusion and meeting was the disaster.

jokes.body: When I was a kid my parents moved a lot,
jokes.punch: but I always found them.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. The setup implies instability/poverty; the punchline flips it — the tragedy isn't that they moved, it's that they were trying to lose him.

jokes.body: I looked up my family tree
jokes.punch: and found out I was the sap.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. Wordplay — 'sap' means both the fluid in a tree and a gullible fool. The genealogy metaphor earns the double meaning.

jokes.body: I get no respect. I played hide and seek.
jokes.punch: They wouldn't even look for me.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. The 'no respect' formula at its most purely desolate — abandonment reframed as a game mechanic.

jokes.body: They say that if you're afraid of something, you should face it.
jokes.punch: But what if you're afraid of a guy with a baseball bat?
jokes.context: Norm Macdonald. Takes the therapeutic cliché and finds the edge case where it becomes catastrophically bad advice. The specificity of 'baseball bat' is key.

jokes.body: In my opinion, if you're going to fight the War on Terror,
jokes.punch: a good place to start would be the Haunted House.
jokes.context: Norm Macdonald. Deflates geopolitical rhetoric by relocating it to a seasonal attraction. The earnest delivery makes it land as if it might actually be a policy proposal.

jokes.body: I'm not one for giving advice, but if you're ever feeling down, just remember:
jokes.punch: you could be a moth.
jokes.context: Norm Macdonald. Anti-inspirational — the consolation is so low it functions as a kind of existential floor. Being a moth is presented as the worst possible condition.

jokes.body: My favorite disease is the one where you forget everything.
jokes.punch: I forgot what it's called.
jokes.context: Norm Macdonald. Perfect self-referential loop — the joke performs its own subject matter. The delivery had to be bone-dry or the structure collapses.

jokes.body: Dialogue & Situational Roleplay (Shane Gillis)
jokes.punch: Build a scene, then inhabit every character in it. The comedian becomes the casting director, director, and all actors simultaneously.
jokes.context: Gillis — George Washington bit. 'You guys ever think about how crazy George Washington was? He was just like a 6'3" freak in a wig. He was like, Alright, let's go kill the King. And everyone was like, Wait — we can do that? He was probably a guy who just walked into rooms and said, Vibes are off. Everyone's short. Get some big guys in here. We're doing a revolution.' The joke lives in the characters, not the setup.
jokes.notes: Key mechanic: the comedian plays both the authority figure AND the confused onlooker. Status gap between them creates the comedy.

jokes.body: Internal Monologue / The Whisper (Jim Gaffigan)
jokes.punch: Voice a second self who is the audience's conscience — critical, uncomfortable, asking the questions no one wants to ask out loud.
jokes.context: Gaffigan — Hot Pockets. 'There's no such thing as a good Hot Pocket. There's only a lava Hot Pocket or a frozen solid Hot Pocket. You take a bite, it destroys the roof of your mouth, and you're like, Well, I'm committed now. [High-pitched voice] He's making us feel guilty about the blooming onion. I came here to laugh, not feel bad about my gluttony.' The whisper IS the audience.
jokes.notes: The whisper externalises the performer's self-doubt. Audience laughs because the whisper says what they're thinking. Gaffigan plays himself as both victim and perpetrator.

jokes.body: Aggressive Realism / Rhetorical Performance (Bernie Mac)
jokes.punch: Assert dominance over the room first, then use that authority to tell uncomfortable truths. The audience laughs because they're being told what's true by someone who cannot be argued with.
jokes.context: Bernie Mac — I Ain't Scared of You. 'I ain't scared of you suckas! You don't know where I've been! I come from a place where you gotta fight to get in and you gotta fight to get out. You think I'm scared of a microphone?' Not a joke structure — a status assertion that earns the right to say anything that follows.
jokes.notes: Compare: Gaffigan talks TO himself (whisper), Gillis talks AS characters, Mac talks AT the audience to force a reaction. Mac's is the rarest — pure performer authority.

jokes.body: Shane Gillis — George Washington
jokes.context:
Full bit. Gillis — dialogue roleplay style. Washington as a jock football coach with a sword.

'You guys ever think about how crazy George Washington was? He was just like a 6'3" freak in a wig. He was just like, Alright, let's go kill the King. And everyone was like, Wait, what? We can do that?

He was probably a guy that just walked into rooms and was like, Vibes are off. Everyone's short. Why are there so many short guys in the Continental Army? Get some big guys in here. We're doing a revolution, I need guys who look like they can bench a carriage.

Imagine being a soldier and Washington just rides by on a white horse like, Looking good, boys. Keep it up. Make sure the wigs are straight. We're about to do some history. He was just a football coach with a sword.'
jokes.notes: Structure: absurd historical reframe → character voice dialogue → punchline comparison. The bit never needs a formal setup — it starts running.

jokes.body: Jim Gaffigan — Hot Pockets
jokes.context:
Full bit. Gaffigan — internal monologue style. Mundane object → cosmic injustice.

'Have you ever had a Hot Pocket? I've never eaten a Hot Pocket and then thought, I'm glad I ate that. I've only ever thought, I'm gonna die. I'm starting to see the tunnel.

[High-pitched voice] I don't like this joke. He's talking about processed frozen meat again.

They have the jingle: Hot Pocket! You think they worked hard on that song? What do you got so far, Bill? Uh... Hot Pocket. That's good. Put it to music.

There's no such thing as a good Hot Pocket. There's only a lava Hot Pocket or a frozen solid Hot Pocket. There is no middle ground. It's a physical impossibility. You take a bite, it destroys the roof of your mouth, and you're like, Well, I'm committed now. I guess I'll just be skinless for a week.'
jokes.notes: The whisper voice appears mid-bit to give the audience permission to enjoy something they feel bad about enjoying. This is the Gaffigan formula: guilt about something trivial, elevated to tragedy.

jokes.body: Jim Gaffigan — The Appetizer
jokes.context:
Full bit. Gaffigan — global perspective shame device.

'It would be embarrassing trying to explain what an appetizer is to someone from a starving country.

Yeah, the appetizer — that's the food we eat before we have our food. No, no, you're thinking of dessert — that's food we have after we have our food. We eat tons of food. Sometimes there's so much we just stick it in a bag and bring it home. Then we throw it out the next day. Maybe give it to the dog.

[High-pitched voice] He's making us feel guilty about the blooming onion. I came here to laugh, not feel bad about my gluttony.'
jokes.notes: The format: state absurd American custom as fact → let the whisper voice do the moral reckoning → laugh at the impossibility of changing.

jokes.body: Bernie Mac — I Ain't Scared of You (Def Comedy Jam)
jokes.context:
Full bit / status-assertion intro. Bernie Mac — rhetorical performance style.

'I ain't scared of you suckas! I'm gonna tell you something right now... I don't care what you say. You don't know me! You don't know where I've been!

I come from a place where you gotta fight to get in and you gotta fight to get out. And you think I'm scared of a microphone? I'm here to make you laugh, and if you don't laugh, I'm gonna talk about your mama!

I'm the Mac! I'm the King of Comedy! I've been doing this for twenty years, and you're gonna sit there and look at me like I'm a stranger? I ain't no stranger! I'm your daddy!'
jokes.notes: This is less a joke and more a social contract. Mac establishes that he will say anything. The audience doesn't laugh at punchlines — they laugh in relief that someone this confident is on their side.

jokes.body: Bernie Mac — The Parenting Bit (Bang-Bang)
jokes.context:
Full bit. Bernie Mac — escalating exaggeration, preacher rhythm.

'I'm taking care of my sister's kids. Three of em. And let me tell you something... kids today are different. They don't have no respect. They look at you and say, You can't hit me! I'll call the police!

I told em, Go ahead! Call em! Tell em to bring a sandwich, cause they're gonna be here a while!

My niece, she's five. She gonna look at me and say, Uncle Bernie, I'm hungry. I said, You hungry? There's a kitchen. Go in there and make yourself a sandwich. She says, I don't know how. I said, Well, you better learn! This ain't a restaurant! I'm the Mac, not McDonald's!'
jokes.notes: Structure: real situation → escalation → impossible demand → punchline that reframes the comedian as institution rather than person. 'I'm the Mac, not McDonald's' is the template.

jokes.body: Bernie Mac — Milk and Cookies Logic
jokes.context:
Full bit. Bernie Mac — transactional logic as parenting philosophy.

'People ask me, Bernie, why you so mean to the kids? I ain't mean! I'm teaching em life! Life is mean!

You can't give a kid everything they want. You give em a cookie, they want milk. You give em milk, they want a napkin. You give em a napkin, they want to go to the bathroom.

I told em, Listen, you got the cookie. That's the end of the transaction! Don't come back to the window asking for upgrades!'
jokes.notes: 'Don't come back to the window asking for upgrades' — one of the cleanest single-line summaries of a philosophical position in standup. The joke earns a permanent rule.

jokes.body: The Escalating Applause Bit (conceptual template)
jokes.context:
A longform structure where the comedian gets applause for increasingly mundane achievements, building to absurdity.

Opening: 'I just want to say — I got dressed today. [pause for appreciation] I know, I know. Socks AND shoes. In the correct order.'

Escalation 1: 'I also ate breakfast. Not just coffee — actual food that required a spoon. The spoon went in the bowl. The bowl did not tip over. I'm not bragging, I'm just — documenting.'

Escalation 2: 'I sent an email. Subject line. Body text. Correct recipient. No attachment that said Attachment. I'm basically running a Fortune 500 company at this point.'

Payoff: 'The bar is on the floor and I am vaulting it. Every single day I come home and I think — I did not die today. And somewhere, someone is not giving me enough credit for that.'
jokes.notes: Template: pick an activity → celebrate it at each step → each celebration is more absurd than the last → final step reframes the whole piece as existential victory. The buildup IS the bit.

jokes.body: The Extended Callback Structure (technique example)
jokes.context:
Longform technique: plant 3 seeds in the first 2 minutes. Pay all 3 off in the final minute.

Seed 1 (minute 1): Offhand mention of your uncle who insists he 'invented' Post-it notes.
Seed 2 (minute 1.5): Bit about how hospitals are too cold.
Seed 3 (minute 2): Observation about people who insist they 'don't dream.'

Callback payoff (final minute): 'My uncle is in the hospital — too cold, obviously, he's in a cardigan — and he's telling the nurse he invented Post-it notes. She writes something on a Post-it and sticks it to his chart. He goes completely still. He looks at me. He goes: I think I might be dreaming.'

All three seeds resolve in one image. The audience feels the construction click into place.
jokes.notes: Rule of three callbacks: plant casually, never call attention to the seeds, pay off fast. The laugh is proportional to how long ago the seed was planted.

variants.body: I've been driving for fifteen years. I still grip the wheel like it might make a run for it.
At some point you'd think muscle memory kicks in. But every parking lot is a fresh negotiation. Every parallel park is a hostage situation I'm not qualified for.
My dad has been cooking the same four meals for forty years. Last week he burned pasta. The water. He burned the water.
Competence is just failure that got bored and stopped showing up. Until it does again.
I've tied my shoes approximately fourteen thousand times. Yesterday one of them just... didn't. I sat there for a full minute. Like we were strangers. Like I owed it an apology.

jokes.body: Core idea: Being bad at something you've done for years.
jokes.context: The same kernel expressed in different structural forms — which one lands best?

variants.body: 'Let me know if you need anything' means nothing. It's punctuation. It ends sentences the same way a period does.
'We should hang out sometime' — noun phrase. Literal translation: I have acknowledged your existence. Do not follow up on this.
'I'll be there in five minutes' is fifteen. 'It's almost done' means I haven't started. 'I was just about to call you' means you caught me.
The 'we should catch up' text. Sent. Read. Both parties now feel like they did catch up.

Imagine if people just said the actual thing. 'I like you but I don't want to make plans.' 'I'm not going to do that but I appreciate being asked.' Society would collapse but at least the texts would be shorter.

jokes.body: Core idea: The gap between what you say and what you mean.
jokes.context: Same social observation — how do you frame the gap between stated and actual intent?

jokes.body: Bernie Mac — The 3 AM Power Struggle
jokes.punch: I ain't scared of you motherf***ers!
jokes.context: Bernie's sister leaves her kids with him. 3 AM, a small child stares him down for snacks. Bernie realizes it's not hunger — it's a power struggle. He locks eyes with a five-year-old and screams his signature line.
jokes.notes: The bit works because the threat is absurd — a grown man declaring war on a kindergartner over bedtime snacks. The punchline lands because Bernie commits to it completely. He's not joking about being scared. He's proving he isn't.

jokes.body: Katt Williams — The Hater Ratio
jokes.punch: Lord, give me an extra hater today, because I'm about to do something big.
jokes.context: People complaining about having haters. Katt's inversion: haters are a metric of success, not a burden. If you have 14 haters, you need 16 by summer. If you have zero, you aren't doing anything worth hating.
jokes.notes: The prayer at the end is the real punchline — asking God for more enemies as a blessing. Reframes hostility as confirmation of importance. Classic status-flip structure.

jokes.body: Shane Gillis — The Civil War Phase
jokes.punch: Standing in a field in Pennsylvania, wearing a tan vest, explaining to your bored wife exactly why the Union's defensive line at Gettysburg was 'actually very impressive.'
jokes.context: Every man hits a phase where he becomes obsessed with history. You don't choose it. One day you're normal. The next you're 40 years old in a tan vest at a battlefield.
jokes.notes: The tan vest is load-bearing detail. It's the most specific possible image of the type — not just 'history guy' but the exact outfit that signals the phase has fully consumed him. Gillis's specificity is the joke.

jokes.body: Louis C.K. — The Miracle of Flight
jokes.punch: You're sitting in a chair, in the sky. Traveling at 500 miles per hour. And you're upset because a YouTube video took ten seconds to load?
jokes.context: People getting angry about bad airplane Wi-Fi. Louis reframes the mundane complaint against the literal miracle of human flight. The Wi-Fi went out. 'This is BS!' You are defying gravity at altitude.
jokes.notes: Structure: establish the complaint as trivial → zoom out to cosmic scale → make the audience feel absurd for agreeing with the complaint. The zoom is everything. 'Chair. In the sky.' Two words that reframe an entire industry.

jokes.body: Bernie Mac — Fearless Comedy (The Hostile Crowd)
jokes.punch: I didn't come here for you to like me! I came here to make you laugh!
jokes.context: Walking out to a skeptical crowd looking at him like he owes them money. Bernie doesn't tell a joke. He barks at them instead.
jokes.notes: Paired with 'I Ain't Scared of You' as status-assertion comedy. The joke isn't the words — it's the delivery of genuine hostility toward the audience. The crowd laughs because being challenged by someone this unafraid is funnier than any setup/punchline.

jokes.body: Katt Williams — The Baby Bentley
jokes.punch: It looks just like a Bentley until a real Bentley pulls up next to it. Then it just looks like a Chrysler that's holding its breath.
jokes.context: People trying to look rich in a Chrysler 300, which resembles a Bentley until you park them side by side.
jokes.notes: 'Holding its breath' is the entire joke. The anthropomorphization of the car as aspirational but self-aware — it knows it isn't what it's pretending to be. Katt's genius is in finding the one phrase that makes an inanimate object pathetic.

jokes.body: Shane Gillis — The Fox News Dad
jokes.punch: You ask him how the grilled cheese is and he pivots into a 20-minute rant about border security before he's even finished his first bite.
jokes.context: Trying to have a normal conversation with a father who has been watching cable news all day. The grilled cheese is the trigger.
jokes.notes: The grilled cheese is perfect. It's the most innocuous possible entry point — not politics, not the news, just a sandwich. The fact that even that gets absorbed into the rant tells you everything about the state he's in. Gillis trusts the setup to do the work.

jokes.body: Louis C.K. — Of Course... But Maybe
jokes.punch: Of course we should help the poor and hungry... but maybe if we just ignored them for a week, the problem would sort of solve itself?
jokes.context: The gap between what people say is right and what they actually think. Louis surfaces the 'horrible person' living inside everyone's brain — the voice that says the true awful thing right after the socially acceptable one.
jokes.notes: The structure is replicable: 'Of course [virtue signal]... but maybe [the thing you actually thought].' It works because the audience recognizes the voice immediately — it's their own. The comedian isn't confessing; he's holding up a mirror.

jokes.body:
THE ENTIRE US STOCK MARKET / AXIOS TWEETS

Visual metaphor read: enormous tilting building = the entire economy. Wooden prop = one casual tweet. The prop is hilariously inadequate for the load.

This is the core visual metaphor engine: ENORMOUS WEIGHT / tiny inadequate support.

Practice with this frame:
— 'My entire sense of self' / 'one compliment from a stranger in 2019'
— 'My sleep schedule' / 'a promise to fix it on Sunday'
— 'Western democracy' / 'strongly-worded letter'
— 'My career plans' / 'a vibe'

The exercise: find a real image (news, meme, architecture, nature). Identify what enormous weight it contains. Identify what implausibly small thing is holding it up. Caption accordingly.

PERSONIFICATION LAB — give anything a perspective

Exercise 1 — The Object Has Opinions:
Subject: parking meter
Inner life: exhausted, underpaid, offended by every quarter
'You have no idea how long I've been standing here.'

Exercise 2 — The Concept Is Tired:
Subject: gravity
Inner life: professionally obligated, slightly resentful
'Do you know how many things I have to hold down? I am a fundamental force. I deserve better.'

Exercise 3 — The Emotion Has a Job:
Subject: mild Sunday dread
Inner life: middle management, clipboard, been here since 4pm
'I'm not saying anything is wrong. I've just scheduled a meeting for 3am to discuss it.'

The skill: find the thing's grievance. Every object, concept, and abstraction has one. Surface it.
jokes.notes: Personification is empathy turned sideways. The funnier the mismatch between subject and interiority, the better the entry point.

jokes.body:
FREE ASSOCIATION CHAIN — 30 second exercise

Seed: UMBRELLA → rain → petrichor → grief smells like that → what does joy smell like → sunscreen → summer → boredom → ten years old → ice cream truck → pavlovian → Ivan Pavlov had a good dog → all dogs deserve tenure → academia is a dog whistle → dog whistle → politicians → what do politicians smell like → old carpet and ambition → ambition is a cologne nobody admits to wearing → Axe body spray but for power → power suits → suits with actual power → Magneto in a boardroom → 'I need those reports by Friday' with metal hands → jazz hands → free jazz is the free association of music → full circle

UMBRELLA to JAZZ in 20 hops. Any two adjacent items are a possible premise.

Practice: pick any word, set 30 seconds, do not stop, do not filter.
jokes.notes: Free association is how you find the weird thing. The first 10 hops are logical. After 10 it gets interesting. Train for hops 10-20.

jokes.body:
SILLINESS EXTRAPOLATION — ketchup exercise

Seed: someone asks if you want ketchup with your fries

Level 1: 'Yes thank you' — baseline, no comedy
Level 2: you say no. The fry person is briefly confused.
Level 3: they haven't heard no in 7 years. They have only one response pathway.
Level 4: small crowd gathers. Manager is called. 'Someone said no to ketchup.'
Level 5: a ceremony. The event is logged. They make a plaque.
Level 6: generations pass. The plaque is all that remains. A religion forms around the ketchupless one.

Most people stop at level 3. Funny people live at level 5.

Practice: take any mundane exchange. Run it to level 6. Then find the level that plays best for your audience.
jokes.notes: Silliness extrapolation is following a logic train past the point of sanity while staying on the rails. The comedy is in the commitment, not the derailment.

jokes.body:
REFRAME ENGINE — being late to a meeting

Your frame: anxious, apologetic, social contract violated
Boss frame: mildly irritated, clock-watching
Child frame: unbothered — is there cake though
Alien frame: fascinating. These creatures assign themselves to locations at precise moments. One failed to manifest. Distress rituals are performed. The late one enters. Distress transfers. No physical harm occurred. They continue.
Dead person frame: you're at a meeting. And you're worried. About being late.
Cat frame: something more interesting happened. This is correct behaviour.
Historian (500 years hence): the micro-ritual of corporate timekeeping

The skill: 5 frames minimum for any situation. The joke usually lives in the alien, the child, or the dead person frame — anything that strips away conditioning and sees the actual behaviour.
jokes.notes: The reframe engine makes the mundane visible again. Everyone around you is doing something completely insane. The alien frame reminds you of that.

jokes.body:
METAPHOR FORGE — Sunday evening dread

Bad (too expected): 'like a shadow falling over the day'
Better (specific): 'like a meeting request appearing in your body'
Better (wrong but true): 'like your skeleton filing a complaint'
Better (character): 'like mild dread clocking in for its shift'
Best (reveals something): 'the weekend is a finite resource and your nervous system is running the cost-benefit'

The forge rule:
1. Expected metaphor
2. Make it specific
3. Make it wrong
4. Make it a character
5. Find what it reveals

Target 2: confidence
— Expected: 'like armour'
— Specific: 'everyone else is also winging it and you've been informed'
— Wrong but true: 'the gap between competence and self-assessment briefly closed'
— Character: 'inner critic took a personal day'
— Reveals: confidence isn't certainty — it's the absence of the voice that questions it
jokes.notes: Good metaphors are wrong in a specific way that reveals truth. Run the forge: expected → specific → wrong → character → reveals.

jokes.body:
FREE ASSOCIATION CHAIN — 30 second exercise

Seed: UMBRELLA → rain → petrichor → grief smells like that → what does joy smell like → sunscreen → summer → boredom → ten years old → ice cream truck → pavlovian → Ivan Pavlov had a good dog → all dogs deserve tenure → academia is a dog whistle → dog whistle → politicians → what do politicians smell like → old carpet and ambition → ambition is a cologne nobody admits to wearing → Axe body spray but for power → power suits → suits with actual power → Magneto in a boardroom → 'I need those reports by Friday' with metal hands → jazz hands → free jazz is the free association of music → full circle

UMBRELLA to JAZZ in 20 hops. Any two adjacent items are a possible premise.

Practice: pick any word, set 30 seconds, do not stop, do not filter.

jokes.body: I told my doctor I broke my arm in two places.
jokes.punch: He told me to stop going to those places.

jokes.body: I used to hate facial hair, but then it grew on me.
A support group for people who can't stop narrating their own lives. Nobody speaks — they just describe the silence.

variants.body: Cemeteries raised burial prices. Blamed cost of living.
A cemetery quietly raised its burial prices this quarter and, when asked to explain, cited the rising cost of living.

jokes.body: A cemetery raised its burial prices and blamed the cost of living.
Self-checkout machines have made us all unpaid employees who get to watch our groceries slowly betray us.
Why did the nihilist cross the road?
jokes.punch: It doesn't matter.

jokes.body: A horror movie but it's just a guy who can't remember if he locked his door, played in real time for 90 minutes.
My therapist told me I have trouble accepting things I can't control.
jokes.punch: We'll see about that.

jokes.body: I'm reading a book about anti-gravity.
jokes.punch: It's impossible to put down.

jokes.body: The most haunted thing in any horror movie is never the ghost — it's the mortgage.
Life is like a box of chocolates.
jokes.punch: It's shorter for fat people.

jokes.body: She will be a heroine.
jokes.punch: Not a hero — but a heroine addict's best friend.

jokes.body: Gayest person award goes to... Homo...
jokes.punch: ...Sapiens.

jokes.body: I just dropped a deuce on Trump...
jokes.punch: ...Tower. In the public bathroom.

jokes.body: Humans are passive plagiarists — we drink from the urethra of ignorance (collective data) and piss out a spin. We don't create, we recombine.
NYPD as a homeless escort patrol. The crime isn't being homeless — it's looking homeless. They're not protecting the public from crime, they're protecting aesthetics from poverty.
Prompt engineering as bending digital reality before the Fun Police arrive to shut it down. The wizard who codes the rules of the game from inside the game.
Brain not keeping up with a conversation — you throw an unbaked cookie into the mix just to say something. The half-baked contribution as social survival.
Politicians use a dusting of truth to pass the cover-book bullshit test. The winding road of bullshit is paved with 10% fact and 90% confident misdirection.

variants.body: 'Stew Pitt Nay Grow'
'Imis Pen, Ice Liquor'

jokes.body: Auditory code: 'Emma Lion Pizza Sheet' — say it fast.
jokes.punch: I'm a lying piece of shit.

jokes.body: Ambiguity Juxtaposition: two conversations happening simultaneously — find the equivocation overlap point where both meanings collide.
Frame Hijacking: mirror an insult back so the target can't tell if you're agreeing or escalating. 'Why, because my balls are bigger than yours?'
The Dundee Award: bestow an absurd, unearned honorific on someone to confuse or disarm. 'As the recipient of the 2024 Dundee Prize for Excellence in Being Wrong...'
The Telescope/Salt Shaker: physical miming of an O-shape leading to an x-rated visual misunderstanding.
'It's Rapetime!' — corrected to 'Harvey Time' (Weinstein). The dark alley bit.
Crass Rebranding: 'Constabulary' → 'Come stab u later'. 'Aspergers' → 'Assbergers'.
The Litter of Babies: gross-out lines engineered to guarantee a hurling reaction from women.
Incongruity-Resolution: brain perceives two incompatible scripts. The joke is the logical mechanism that resolves them. The laugh is the release of that tension.
Backloading: the reveal word always goes at the END of the sentence. Never step on the laugh by burying the funny word mid-sentence.
Semantic Fan-Out: pick a word like 'Run' or 'Dates' — list every possible definition — find the switch point where meanings collide unexpectedly.
The Purple Elephant: call out the antisocial pink elephant in the room without making the target an outcast. Comedy as social surgery.
Why did the little girl fall off the swing?
jokes.punch: Because she had no arms.

jokes.body: I spilled spot remover on my dog.
jokes.punch: Now he's gone.
jokes.context: Steven Wright. Takes the literal interpretation of a product name and applies it to a physical entity — absurdist erasure.
jokes.notes: Deadpan delivery essential. No setup signaling.

jokes.body: I busted a mirror and got seven years bad luck.
jokes.punch: But my lawyer thinks he can get me five.
jokes.context: Steven Wright. Blends folklore/superstition with banality of the modern legal system.
jokes.notes: The word 'five' lands the joke — pure backloading.

jokes.body: Everywhere is within walking distance...
jokes.punch: ...if you have the time.
jokes.context: Steven Wright. Tautology masquerading as profound realization — technically true, practically useless.

jokes.body: If at first you don't succeed...
jokes.punch: ...then skydiving isn't for you.
jokes.context: Steven Wright. Paraprosdokian — subverts motivational cliché by applying it to a high-stakes scenario where failure is instantly fatal.
jokes.notes: Classic backloading. 'Skydiving' does all the work.

jokes.body: I put instant coffee in a microwave...
jokes.punch: ...and almost went back in time.
jokes.context: Steven Wright. Surrealism — combines two symbols of modern convenience to suggest exponential accumulation of speed.

jokes.body: I'm sick of following my dreams.
jokes.punch: I'm just going to ask them where they're going and hook up with them later.
jokes.context: Mitch Hedberg. Personifies the abstract concept of a 'dream,' treating an existential pursuit as a casual social appointment.
jokes.notes: The delivery drag on 'later' matters enormously.

jokes.body: I don't have a girlfriend.
jokes.punch: I just have someone who would be really mad if they heard me say that.
jokes.context: Mitch Hedberg. Syntactical evasion — redefines a romantic relationship purely through the lens of conflict avoidance.

jokes.body: I know I'm ugly. When my dog found out we look alike...
jokes.punch: ...he killed himself.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. Self-deprecating. Hyperbolic — attributes complex psychological vanity and suicidal ideation to an animal.

jokes.body: My wife's a terrible cook. If I leave dental floss in the kitchen...
jokes.punch: ...the cockroaches hang themselves.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. Escalates marital complaints into the grotesque to emphasize a toxic domestic environment.

jokes.body: I went to a fight the other night...
jokes.punch: ...and a hockey game broke out.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. Structural inversion — swaps the primary and secondary events of a familiar observation.

jokes.body: I rang up British Telecom and said: 'I want to report a nuisance caller.'
jokes.punch: He said: 'Not you again.'
jokes.context: Tim Vine. Reverses the subject-object relationship — audience assumes the comedian is the victim, but he's the perpetrator.

jokes.body: Here's a picture of me with REM.
jokes.punch: That's me in the corner.
jokes.context: Milton Jones. Relies on shared cultural knowledge of 'Losing My Religion' by REM — 'That's me in the corner' is a lyric.

jokes.body: Two fish in a tank. One says:
jokes.punch: 'How do you drive this thing?'
jokes.context: Peter Kay. Lexical ambiguity pun — pivots on the dual meaning of 'tank' (fish tank vs. military tank).

jokes.body: If God had written the Bible, the first line should have been:
jokes.punch: 'It's round.'
jokes.context: Eddie Izzard. Satirizes religious resistance to science — a divine author would have prioritized basic cosmology.

jokes.body: I worry about ridiculous things... how does a guy who drives a snowplough...
jokes.punch: ...get to work in the morning?
jokes.context: Billy Connolly. Highlights logistical paradoxes of modern infrastructure and the circular dependency of emergency services.

jokes.body: I used to go out with a giraffe. People said 'he takes up too much room'...
jokes.punch: 'Well he can take his hat off for a start!'
jokes.context: Paul Merton. Introduces absurd premise but grounds the conflict in mundane cinematic etiquette.

jokes.body: We weren't very religious. On Hanukkah...
jokes.punch: ...my mother had our menorah on a dimmer.
jokes.context: Richard Lewis. Synthesizes modern electrical convenience with ancient tradition to illustrate lack of orthodox commitment.

jokes.body: The first guy who persuaded a blind person they needed sunglasses...
jokes.punch: ...he must have been a hell of a salesman.
jokes.context: Jimmy Carr. Explores absurdity of an accepted social norm by pointing out a logical inconsistency.

jokes.body: My girlfriend is absolutely beautiful. Body like a Greek statue —
jokes.punch: ...completely pale, no arms.
jokes.context: Phil Wang. Uses a common simile then pivots to the literal, ruined physical state of actual antiquities.
jokes.notes: The pause before 'completely pale' sets up the misdirection.

jokes.body: A 90-year-old's friends hire a lady of the evening. She says: 'I'm here for super sex.' He replies:
jokes.punch: 'I'll take the soup.'
jokes.context: Jane Lynch. Pivots on phonetic ambiguity ('super sex' vs 'soup or sex') and subversion of virility tropes in aging.

jokes.body: A string tries to get a drink at a bar. He comes back frayed and says:
jokes.punch: 'No, I'm a frayed knot.'
jokes.context: Kumail Nanjiani. Classic pun — homophonic relationship between 'afraid not' and the physical state of frayed string.

jokes.body: Rene Descartes is asked if he wants an hors d'oeuvre. He says: 'I think not'...
jokes.punch: ...and disappears.
jokes.context: Aasif Mandvi. Intellectual pun requiring Cartesian philosophy — 'I think therefore I am.' By ceasing to think, he ceases to exist.

jokes.body: Ron Swanson on eating at a bowling alley:
jokes.punch: 'When I eat, it is the food that is scared.'
jokes.context: Mike Schur (Parks & Rec). Personifies sustenance to establish hyper-masculinity — transforms passive act of eating into domination.

jokes.body: A man pushes another off a bridge after learning he belongs to a different Baptist council from 1912.
jokes.context: Eugene Mirman. Satirizes the narcissism of minor differences — how violent hatred stems from imperceptible theological distinctions.

jokes.body: My father was a night watchman, but he was a victim of technology.
jokes.punch: He was replaced by a lock.
jokes.context: Colin Quinn. Highlights absurdity of a human occupation so simple it can be replaced by a basic mechanical object.

jokes.body: The Olympic Silver Medal. Of all the losers...
jokes.punch: ...you came in first! You're the number one loser!
jokes.context: Jerry Seinfeld. Inverts traditional hierarchies — identifies the unique agony of absolute proximity to ultimate victory.

jokes.body: Hot Pockets. I paid for that? It's frozen.
jokes.punch: But it can be served boiling lava hot.
jokes.context: Jim Gaffigan. Commentary on post-consumption regret and inherent contradictions of modern processed food.

jokes.body: The only knife I have seen in Hampstead...
jokes.punch: ...was a palette knife held by an oil painter.
jokes.context: Ricky Gervais. Generates humor from the perspective of an outsider observing the absolute absence of crime in elite circles.

jokes.body: I think all bullets should cost five thousand dollars.
jokes.punch: 'Man, I was mad, but not five-thousand-dollar mad.'
jokes.context: Chris Rock. Reframes violent social crisis through the lens of capitalism and supply-and-demand.

jokes.body: A lot of people don't like bumper stickers. I don't mind bumper stickers. To me a bumper sticker is a shortcut.
jokes.punch: It's like a little sign that says 'Hey, let's never hang out.'
jokes.context: Demetri Martin. Reframes a decorative object as a social screening tool — the joke lands on the word 'shortcut' then pays it off.

jokes.body: I bought some batteries,
jokes.punch: but they weren't included.
jokes.context: Jimmy Carr. Exploits the familiar packaging phrase 'batteries not included' by reversing the context — the batteries ARE the purchase.

jokes.body: My mother always used to say: The older you get, the better you get.
jokes.punch: Unless you're a banana.
jokes.context: Anthony Jeselnik. Classic paraprosdokian — warm maternal wisdom undercut by a single absurd exception that reframes everything.

jokes.body: It says here in this history book that luckily, the good guys have won every single time.
jokes.punch: What are the odds?
jokes.context: Norm Macdonald. Deadpan skewering of historical revisionism — history is always written by the winners, so the outcome is 100% predictable.

jokes.body: I'm not a fatalist,
jokes.punch: but even if I were, what could I do about it?
jokes.context: Emo Philips. Philosophical trap — denying fatalism while structurally demonstrating it. The punchline is the proof of the premise.

jokes.body: A day without sunshine is like,
jokes.punch: you know, night.
jokes.context: Steve Martin. Subverts the inspirational cliché by completing it with an obvious, literal answer — the emptiness is the point.

jokes.body: Before you criticize someone, you should walk a mile in their shoes. That way when you criticize them,
jokes.punch: you are a mile away from them and you have their shoes.
jokes.context: Jack Handey. Takes the empathy cliché literally and extracts a tactical advantage — the punchline is logistical, not moral.

jokes.body: Guns don't kill people, people kill people.
jokes.punch: And monkeys do too (if they have a gun).
jokes.context: Eddie Izzard. Adds a technically accurate exception to a political platitude — the absurdity of the qualifier exposes the absurdity of the original.

jokes.body: I have a lot of growing up to do.
jokes.punch: I realized that the other day inside my fort.
jokes.context: Zach Galifianakis. Self-deprecation via setting — the revelation of immaturity happens in the least mature possible location.

jokes.body: I'm a vegetarian. I'm not strict,
jokes.punch: I eat pork, beef, and chicken. But I'm not a strict vegetarian.
jokes.context: Jay London. Hollow self-labeling — the qualifier 'strict' is used to absorb the entire contradiction rather than resolve it.

jokes.body: I told my psychiatrist that everyone hates me.
jokes.punch: He said I was being ridiculous — everyone hasn't met me yet.
jokes.context: Rodney Dangerfield. The therapist's correction makes things worse — reframes the universal rejection as not yet complete.

jokes.body: A study says that having a lot of friends makes you live longer.
jokes.punch: Which explains why I'm going to die on Thursday.
jokes.context: Conan O'Brien. Applies a statistical finding to a specific personal timeline — the precision of 'Thursday' sells it.

jokes.body: I don't exercise.
jokes.punch: If God had wanted me to bend over, he would have put diamonds on the floor.
jokes.context: Joan Rivers. Theological justification for laziness — elevates personal sloth to a matter of divine intent.

jokes.body: I'm in my late thirties, which is the age where you have to start doing things like 'taking care of your body'
jokes.punch: because otherwise, you die.
jokes.context: Mike Birbiglia. Strips away euphemism — the vague cultural pressure to 'take care of yourself' is bluntly literalized as survival.

jokes.body: I want my children to have all the things I couldn't afford.
jokes.punch: Then I want to move in with them.
jokes.context: Phyllis Diller. Generational sacrifice reframed as a long-game financial strategy — parental love as delayed self-interest.

jokes.body: I'm at that age where my friends are either getting married or getting DUIs, and it's like, which path am I going down?
jokes.punch: Obviously neither, I'm staying inside.
jokes.context: Taylor Tomlinson. Sets up a binary life choice then rejects both — the third option is social withdrawal, delivered as contentment.

jokes.body: Outside of a dog, a book is man's best friend.
jokes.punch: Inside of a dog, it's too dark to read.
jokes.context: Groucho Marx. Classic paraprosdokian — 'outside of' is idiomatic in the setup but then taken spatially/literally in the punchline.

jokes.body: I'm giving up acting to become a monk.
jokes.punch: I've had it with this habit.
jokes.context: Gary Delaney. 'Habit' does double duty — monastic robe and a thing one is fed up with. The decision to become a monk generates the punchline word.

jokes.body: I decided to sell my Hoover.
jokes.punch: Well, it was just collecting dust.
jokes.context: Tim Vine. 'Collecting dust' is both the literal failure of a vacuum cleaner and the idiom for abandonment — the punchline is the reason and the joke simultaneously.

jokes.body: I used to be a plastic surgeon.
jokes.punch: Which just goes to show, you never know what someone is made of.
jokes.context: Stewart Francis. 'What someone is made of' is idiomatic (character/resolve) but here cashed out literally — a plastic surgeon knows exactly what people are made of.

jokes.body: About a month before he died, my grandfather covered his back with lard.
jokes.punch: After that, he went downhill fast.
jokes.context: Milton Jones. 'Went downhill fast' is idiomatic (deteriorated quickly) but the lard on the back generates a literal physical image of lubricated downward momentum.

jokes.body: To err is human,
jokes.punch: but it feels divine.
jokes.context: Mae West. Inverts Pope's 'To err is human, to forgive divine' — strips the moral imperative and replaces it with sensual pleasure. The pivot is on 'divine' shifting from theological to hedonistic.

videos.title: It's Always Sunny - Frank Freaks Out

examples: That's the most cockamamie plan I've ever heard — and I once watched a man try to open a jar with his elbow for twenty minutes.
His cockamamie theories about pigeons running the government somehow got more convincing the longer he talked.
vocab.notes: Means absurd, implausible, poorly thought out. Perfect for dismissing nonsense with style.

examples: He walks into every meeting like a prancing pony — all confidence, no destination.
I told him to relax and he hit me with the full prancing pony entrance.
vocab.notes: Someone who moves or behaves with exaggerated self-importance or showmanship. High-step energy.

examples: He bloviates for forty minutes and somehow says less than a fortune cookie.
I don't mind public speakers. I mind people who bloviate while looking directly into your soul.
vocab.notes: To talk at length in a pompous or empty way. The sound of the word does half the work.

examples: She's an absolute flibbertigibbet — three conversations at once, none of them finished.
I was trying to explain the situation but he went full flibbertigibbet on me.
vocab.notes: A frivolous, flighty, or excessively talkative person. Maximum silliness per syllable ratio.

examples: He came in with some mighty strong opinions for someone who just discovered the topic yesterday.
That's a mighty bold claim from a man eating a gas station sandwich.
vocab.notes: Conveys scale, force, or audacity. Works deadpan — undersell the delivery, let the word carry weight.

examples: Whatcha mean the restaurant closed? I made a reservation two years ago.
Whatcha mean that's not how taxes work? I've been filing like this for a decade.
vocab.notes: Expresses genuine or performative disbelief. Works best with a pause before it. Streetwise, reactive energy.

examples: He walked in big dick swinging like he invented the concept of a Monday meeting.
There's a certain kind of consultant who comes in big dick swinging and leaves with your budget and your dignity.
vocab.notes: Walking into a situation with maximum unearned confidence. The audacity of presence without credentials.

examples: My nephew is a lil gremlin baby — adorable, chaotic, no rules apply to him.
3am and my brain is being a lil gremlin baby again, serving up every embarrassing memory from 2009.
vocab.notes: A small creature operating outside of normal social contracts. Term of chaotic affection. Also works for yourself at your worst.

examples: They redesigned the app and now it's a grotesque monstrosity of forty tabs and no back button.
I made a smoothie with whatever was in the fridge. It was a grotesque monstrosity. I drank it twice.
vocab.notes: Maximum dramatic condemnation. The gap between the word and what it's applied to is the joke. Use on small things.

examples: The slideshow was pure eye candy — beautiful charts, zero information.
He described himself as eye candy. I appreciated his commitment to the bit.
vocab.notes: Visually appealing, often with the implication that substance is absent. Can be affectionate or cutting.

examples: The menu description was mouth watering. The actual dish was a beige situation.
He described his plan with mouth watering detail, then revealed the budget was eleven dollars.
vocab.notes: Works best when the reality undercuts the hype. Build up the anticipation then land flat.

examples: We finally got the printer to work. Huzzah. Huzzah I say.
Huzzah — the meeting ended four minutes early. Mark this day in the chronicles.
vocab.notes: Archaic cheer. Deeply ironic when applied to modern mundane victories. Medieval energy for 21st century nothing.

examples: I found a twenty in my old jacket. Booyah. That's a retirement plan right there.
Booyah — I remembered to hydrate before noon. Personal record.
vocab.notes: 90s victory call. Ironic sincerity is the move — deliver it like you mean it on something small.

examples: He asked me to pass the salt. "Of course, ur majesty." He didn't like that.
She sent a follow-up email to her follow-up email. Ur majesty is not to be kept waiting.
vocab.notes: Sarcastic deference to someone acting royally entitled. The "ur" keeps it modern, the "majesty" lands the hit.

examples: He described his dating profile strategy. Boo, the ewww.
The catering served warm potato salad. Boo. The ewww.
vocab.notes: Double condemnation — the boo and the ewww hit from different angles. Disgust plus rejection. Works as a complete sentence.

examples: After six weeks of "treating myself" I emerged a fattyboomflabby triumphant.
He said the word fattyboomflabby in a pitch meeting and somehow got the contract.
vocab.notes: Pure phonetic delight. Affectionate self-destruction. The word itself sounds like what it describes — maximum wobble energy.

examples: We're not influencers. We're fattyboom baddies and that's a different category entirely.
The fattyboom baddies have arrived and we are not accepting feedback at this time.
vocab.notes: Reclaimed confidence. Size positivity with swag. Works as a collective noun — solidarity energy.

examples: She moved into what she describes as a big boobie bungalow. Three bedrooms, good light, correct priorities.
The airbnb listing said cozy cottage. Reader, it was a big boobie bungalow.
vocab.notes: Architecture meets body positivity meets absurdism. The specificity of "bungalow" makes it. Suburban, cheerful, unashamed.

vocab.word: Lube for the cog in the sexmachine
examples: He positions himself as the lube for the cog in the sexmachine of this organisation. HR has thoughts.
Every team needs a vibe person. The lube for the cog in the sexmachine. I have volunteered.
vocab.notes: Mechanical metaphor goes full blue. Works as a role description. Say it with complete professionalism and watch the room react.

examples: Woe betide the businessman who assumes the room agrees with him just because no one spoke up.
Woe betide anyone who touches my leftovers. This is not a joke. This is a warning.
vocab.notes: Old English formal curse structure. The contrast between the archaic phrasing and the modern grievance is the engine. Great for elevated petty.

examples: The 6am email senders are ghouls. Every single one of them.
The ghouls are at the networking event again, wearing lanyards and asking about synergies.
vocab.notes: Anyone operating outside the normal human contract — usually corporate, bureaucratic, or chronologically unacceptable. Said with weary recognition.

examples: He prescribed himself a shortstack remedy — pancakes, maple syrup, zero accountability.
The cure for a bad week is a shortstack remedy. Dense, warm, unreasonably effective.
vocab.notes: A small but potent fix. The shortstack is deceptively filling — compact solution with outsized results. Works for any low-effort high-impact solution.

examples: He arrived at the meeting like a dildo ramjet — fast, impractical, and completely unrequested.
The proposal went through the boardroom like a dildo ramjet. Nobody stopped it. Nobody knew how.
vocab.notes: Unstoppable forward momentum combined with questionable utility. Something that barrels through opposition purely on kinetic confidence.

examples: He sat in the corner wearing the dunce cap of his own opinions.
Every room has a dunce. Every meeting has two. Every Zoom call has an entire dunce ecosystem.
vocab.notes: Beautifully old-fashioned condemnation. The word itself sounds like the concept — blunt, round, hollow. Deploy with patience.

examples: He approached the situation like a knuckledragging troglodyte and somehow got promoted.
The comment section was a rich ecosystem of knuckledragging troglodytes finding community.
vocab.notes: Maximum evolutionary contempt. The double compound makes it ceremonial — not casual insult but formal indictment. Reserve for deserving targets.

examples: He liked every single one of her posts going back to 2016. Absolute thirst bucket behaviour.
She walked in like a thirst bucket at a hydration convention — everyone noticed, no one was surprised.
vocab.notes: Someone leaking desire from every pore, usually online. The "bucket" framing implies capacity, volume, spillage. Affectionate or devastating depending on delivery.

examples: Stop lollygagging and get in the van.
He spent forty minutes lollygagging in the cereal aisle, weighing his options like a man with nowhere to be and nothing to lose.
vocab.notes: Wasting time in a pleasurable or aimless way. One of the great words — sounds exactly like what it means. Maximum dawdle energy.

examples: She entered the gala like a full tranny boom blammy — the room didn't know what happened but it knew something had.
I asked for subtle. I got tranny boom blammy. I should have been clearer.
vocab.notes: Explosive, gender-chaotic arrival energy. Someone who hits the room with maximum force and no apologies. See also: funwordizer examples.

vocab.word: Ur Chilly So Here's Some Chili
examples: He looked cold and sad so she handed him a bowl and said ur chilly so here's some chili. He cried. It helped.
I don't have emotional support. I have ur chilly so here's some chili. It's the same thing.
vocab.notes: Phonetic swap as act of care. The wrong solution that's somehow exactly right. Warmth delivered incorrectly but sincerely.

examples: They didn't fix the problem, they just gave the whole department a lubebotomy and sent them home.
He came out of the board meeting looking like he'd had a lubebotomy. Technically fine. Fundamentally different.
vocab.notes: Funwordized "lobotomy" — a procedure that fixes things from the wrong end. Medical gravity applied to undignified processes. See funwordizer lane.

examples: He walked in wearing a suit made of leather and I said heilige schnitzel under my breath.
She revealed the final bill. Heilige schnitzel. I sat down.
vocab.notes: German exclamation — literally "holy schnitzel." Delivers the force of a swear word with the absurdity of a breaded veal cutlet. Maximum impact, minimum offense. Works in any language because nobody knows what it means.

examples: The comments section is just hall monitors all the way down.
He's a hall monitor energy type — technically correct, deeply resented, operating on borrowed authority.
vocab.notes: People who enforce rules they didn't make in spaces that don't belong to them, with an enthusiasm that only makes sense if the badge is their whole identity. Works as a noun, adjective, or energy.
